attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic requesting help to quit smoking. She has been smoking 20 cigarettes per day for the past 25 years and has attempted to quit twice before using nicotine gum, but relapsed within a week each time. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She expresses strong motivation to quit but is concerned about withdrawal symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
For this patient seeking smoking cessation support, the most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention is combination nicotine patch and gum. This approach combines a long-acting nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) for background craving control with a short-acting NRT for breakthrough cravings, which is more effective than single NRT products.

Nicotine patch alone or nicotine gum alone are less effective than combination NRT. Given the patient's previous unsuccessful attempts with nicotine gum, a combination approach is more likely to be successful.

Bupropion is an effective smoking cessation aid but is generally considered second-line due to its side effect profile and contraindications. It may be considered if NRT is ineffective or unsuitable.

Varenicline is the most effective single pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation. However, it is typically reserved for patients who have failed NRT or have contraindications to NRT, due to its higher cost and potential side effects.

It is important to note that pharmacological interventions should always be combined with behavioural support to maximise the chances of successful smoking cessation. This includes counselling on coping strategies, identifying triggers, and setting a quit date. Regular follow-up appointments should be scheduled to monitor progress and provide ongoing support.

The choice of smoking cessation aid should be tailored to the individual patient, considering factors such as previous quit attempts, contraindications, patient preference, and cost. In this case, combination NRT offers a good balance of efficacy, safety, and familiarity for the patient.
learningPoints: Combination nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) is more effective than single NRT for smoking cessation.
Varenicline is the most effective single pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation.
Behavioural support significantly increases the success rate of smoking cessation attempts.
references: Zwar NA. Smoking cessation. Aust J Gen Pract. 2020; 49:474-81.
